food_name: Beef Noodle Soup, Chinese Style (Braised Beef Brisket, Flat Rice Noodles)

local_names.romanized_local: Ngau Nam Min (Cantonese); Niu Rou Mian (Mandarin)

food_subgroup: Flat rice noodles in dark beef bone broth with braised beef brisket and tendon

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Chinese Beef Noodle Soup. Closest parent is a noodle soup with braised beef and bone broth. No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Flat rice noodles in braised beef bone broth with braised beef brisket and tendon, soy and star anise seasoned
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CFA is the closest FoodEx2 parent for noodle-based soup dishes; no exact code exists for this specific Chinese beef noodle preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific dish. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Chinese beef noodle soup weighs approximately 500g, comprising roughly 150g flat rice noodles, 200g beef bone broth, and 150g braised beef brisket and tendon.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Noodle Soup (Chinese style) — HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chinese-style beef noodle soup. All per-100g values are taken from this verified record. HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — flat rice noodles (hor fun) analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: Flat rice noodles (hor fun) have a documented GI of approximately 68–75 in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 68 (medium) applied as the conservative lower bound for this dish. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (68 × 60g carbohydrate in 500g serving) / 100 = 40.8. The high protein content from braised beef brisket provides meaningful GI buffering in a mixed meal context.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — 420mg per 100g; a 500g bowl delivers approximately 2100mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily recommended maximum of 2000mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid consuming the full beef bone broth, request a low-sodium or lightly seasoned 'qing tang' broth, and limit portion size to half a bowl; leaving the broth in the bowl is the single most effective sodium-reduction strategy for this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate-to-high GI flat rice noodles (GI ~68) with a glycemic load of 40.8 per 500g serving; however, the high protein content (9.2g/100g) from braised beef brisket provides significant GI buffering in a mixed meal.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should choose a smaller noodle portion (100–120g cooked noodles), increase the proportion of beef and broth, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to reduce the glycemic load per meal to a clinically acceptable range.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.2g/100g) and high sodium (420mg/100g) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; cholesterol is 38mg/100g, which is moderate for a beef-based dish.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 300–400g, avoid drinking the full broth to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake, and choose lean brisket over tendon-heavy portions to minimise saturated fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef provides highly bioavailable haem zinc (3.5mg/100g) and haem iron (2.2mg/100g); beef tendon is rich in type II collagen, the predominant collagen in articular cartilage.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is beneficial for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high bioavailable zinc, vitamin B12 (1.5mcg/100g), and collagen precursors from beef tendon that may support joint cartilage maintenance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese Beef Noodle Soup (牛肉麵 / 牛肉面) is a deeply satisfying Singaporean Chinese bowl dish consisting of flat rice noodles (hor fun) or wheat noodles immersed in a rich, dark, aromatic beef bone broth flavoured with soy sauce, star anise, garlic, and rock sugar, topped with generous slices of slow-braised beef brisket and chewy, gelatinous beef tendon. The broth is simmered for several hours until it develops a deep mahogany colour and complex, intensely beefy flavour. The combination of tender, flavourful brisket and the collagen-rich tendon is a hallmark of the dish. Popular at Singaporean Chinese restaurants and zi char stalls, particularly in the Cantonese 'ngau nam min' style. Nutritionally, this dish is a high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate meal providing 108 kcal per 100g. It is clinically significant for rheumatoid arthritis patients: beef provides one of the highest bioavailable zinc sources among common foods (3.5mg/100g), and beef tendon provides type II collagen precursors that support joint cartilage maintenance. The primary clinical concern is sodium — 420mg/100g from the long-simmered soy-seasoned broth — making it unsuitable for hypertensive patients unless the broth is left unconsumed. The dish is halal and dairy-free, making it accessible across Singapore's multicultural dining population.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. All per-100g values are verified and complete. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous food (flat rice noodles, GI ~68), which is an approved GI source. Confidence is high as the exact dish is represented in the HPB database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite soup dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on flat rice noodles (hor fun) from the University of Sydney GI Database; the dish GI may vary depending on noodle type (flat rice vs wheat) and broth composition. GL per 500g serving = (68 × 60g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 40.8. The ethnic_tags field in the input contained 'western' which appears to be a data entry error for a Chinese dish; this has been corrected to Chinese/Singaporean Chinese in the output. Two default portions were present in the input (600g and 500g); the 500g verified portion has been used as the canonical serving size. Vitamin A reported as 5.0 mcg RAE per 100g.
